titleStmt.title: Letter from William Tyrrell Jr. to Victoria Mary Tyrrell, 6 November 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Tyrrell Jr.

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
The letters of William Upton Tyrrell ('Willie') to his father William J.H. Tyrrell, mother Elizabeth, sister Minnie and brother Robert are a notable example of wartime correspondence. The letters - nearly 200 in total - are mostly written by William to his family while in active service with the Royal Irish Rifles in France. Willie discusses his life and the lives of other soldiers in the trenches, as well as life at Ballindoolin, the family estate.
The family name 'Tyrrell' is sometimes misspelled 'Tyrell' in correspondence to the family.
Summary of the present letter:
Miss Ball sent Willie an Irish Life magazine with a cartoon of two cats marked. One cat was an R.A.M.C. doctor and the other a soldier asking for sick leave. A young American officer is staying with William's regiment. The American is amazed they are sleeping in beds and eating five-course meals. Leave has been extended.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Tuesday 6/11/17 My dear Minnie Thank you very much
indeed for your letter of 1st which
came yesterday - I really must
start writing to you in French but
I have not enough to say today
to make it worth while!! I am terribly shocked at Miss
Bells conduct. She sent me an
Irish Life and in it marked in
red pencil a horrid cartoon
about two cats. One cat doing
R A M C doctor and the other cat
asking for sick leave. I will have to get some
horrid post card to send her. That
will settle her perhaps. I don't know if I mentioned
yesterday that the new writing pad
arrived at a most oppertune moment
We have a young American officer
attached to us here just to see
what things are like. He seems
a very nice fellow. He lives with
me in my dug out. He came up
with ideas which were all wrong
He had an idea that he was
going to somewhere where there was
a war on. So he was rather
astonished when he saw me
putting on pyjamas and getting in
to my bed comfortably at ten
o clock last night. Of course this
is not in the front line which
makes a considerale difference.
Also he expected I think to live
on bully I beef and dog
biscuits so a 5 course dinner
rather astonished him. There is no doubt I struck luck
when I was put on signalling officer
for this tour. Of course I will go
back to my company as soon as
ever we go out. Young the proper
sigs officer is just having a
rest. No one knows why as
he has just come back off
leave but I am jolly glad he
is getting a rest. Leave by the way now is
14 days instead of 10 but as
we always got 12 days for
Ireland it wont make so
very much difference. I see by yesterdays papers that
there has been some
excitement at Newbridge over
a meeting The Times had a long
story about it. I wrote Bobby a decent letter last
night as I see he says he
has not heard from me for
a long time. I write to him at
least twice a week Weather still warm but dark
The evenings are getting awfully
short. It is pitch dark about
five oclock. No news, I will write again
tomorrow. Very best love to all from
Your most affec brother
Willie
